title: The Income Statement: Revenue to Net Income
html:
The waterfall from sales to profit

The income statement (also called the profit-and-loss statement, or P&L) answers one question: over a period of time — a quarter or a year — how much did the company sell, what did it cost to produce that, and what was left over? Unlike the balance sheet, which is a snapshot at a single instant, the income statement covers a span. Read it as a waterfall: you start at the top with revenue and subtract costs in tiers until you reach the bottom line, net income.

The discipline of reading it is to never look only at the bottom number. Each subtotal along the way — gross profit, operating income, pre-tax income, net income — tells you something different about the business. A company can grow revenue while its margins collapse; it can post a fat net income on a one-time gain while its real operations bleed. The line items are where the story lives.

The four tiers

  • Revenue (top line) — what the company billed customers for goods or services delivered. Also called sales or turnover.
  • Cost of goods sold (COGS) — the direct cost of producing what was sold: raw materials, direct labor, factory overhead. Revenue − COGS = Gross profit.
  • Operating expenses (opex) — the cost of running the business that isn't tied to a specific unit: research and development (R&D), selling, general and administrative (SG&A), depreciation. Gross profit − opex = Operating income (also called EBIT, earnings before interest and taxes).
  • Interest and taxes — the cost of debt and the government's cut. Operating income − interest − tax = Net income (the bottom line).
Definition.
Margin means a profit number expressed as a percent of revenue. Gross margin = gross profit ÷ revenue; operating margin = operating income ÷ revenue; net margin = net income ÷ revenue. Margins let you compare a $50M company to a $50B one on the same footing.

Sample Co, fiscal year

Let's walk a full statement for our recurring example company. Every number is in millions of dollars ($M).

Sample Co — Income Statement ($M)
Revenue 5,000 Cost of goods sold (3,000) = Gross profit 2,000 (40.0% gross margin) R&D (400) SG&A (700) = Operating income (EBIT) 900 (18.0% operating margin) Interest expense (90) = Pre-tax income 810 Income tax (190) = Net income 620 (12.4% net margin)

Trace it: $5,000M revenue minus $3,000M COGS gives $2,000M gross profit. Opex of $1,100M (the $400M R&D plus $700M SG&A) leaves $900M operating income. Subtract $90M interest and you get $810M pre-tax; the $190M tax bill (a 23.5% effective rate) leaves $620M net income.

What each margin tells you

The 40% gross margin says that for every dollar of product sold, 40 cents remains after the direct cost of making it. High gross margins (software, luxury brands) give a company room to spend on growth; thin gross margins (groceries, airlines) leave almost none. The drop from 40% gross to 18% operating reveals how much overhead the business carries. The further drop to 12.4% net is the bite of interest and tax.

Insight.
EPS (earnings per share) = net income ÷ shares outstanding. If Sample Co has 500M shares, EPS = $620M ÷ 500M = $1.24. EPS is what headlines quote and what P/E ratios use, because it normalizes profit to a per-share basis investors can price.

Watch for the non-operating noise

Real statements include lines that aren't part of the core business: a one-time gain from selling a building, a restructuring charge, an impairment write-down. These hit net income but don't recur. That's why analysts separate operating income (the engine) from the bottom line. A company that reports rising net income purely because of a tax benefit or an asset sale has not actually gotten better at its business.

Pitfall.
Beware companies that emphasize adjusted or non-GAAP earnings that strip out "one-time" costs quarter after quarter. If stock-based compensation, restructuring, or "transformation" charges appear every single period, they aren't one-time — they're real costs being hidden.
▸Try It

Build the waterfall yourself and compute every margin for a fictional firm.

  1. Start with revenue of $8,000M and COGS of $5,200M. Compute gross profit and gross margin.
  2. Subtract R&D $500M and SG&A $1,100M to get operating income and operating margin.
  3. Subtract interest $120M and tax $300M to get net income and net margin.
  4. With 400M shares outstanding, compute EPS. Compare your net margin to Sample Co's 12.4% — is this firm more or less profitable per dollar of sales?

title: The Balance Sheet: Assets, Liabilities, Equity
html:
A photograph, not a movie

If the income statement is a movie of a period, the balance sheet is a photograph taken at one instant — the last day of the quarter or year. It lists everything the company owns (assets), everything it owes (liabilities), and what's left over for shareholders (equity). The whole thing is built on a single identity that must always hold:

The accounting equation.
Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Everything a company controls was financed either by borrowing (liabilities) or by owners (equity). The two sides balance by construction — hence "balance" sheet. If they don't balance, something is wrong with the books.

The three blocks

  • Assets — resources the company controls that have value. Split into current (cash, receivables, inventory — expected to convert to cash within a year) and non-current (property, plant and equipment, goodwill, intangibles).
  • Liabilities — obligations the company owes. Split into current (accounts payable, short-term debt, accrued expenses — due within a year) and non-current (long-term debt, pension obligations).
  • Equity — the residual: assets minus liabilities. Includes paid-in capital (money raised by issuing stock) and retained earnings (cumulative profits kept in the business rather than paid out).

Sample Co's balance sheet

Sample Co — Balance Sheet ($M)
ASSETS Cash & equivalents 600 Accounts receivable 625 Inventory 500 Other current assets 375 Total current assets 2,100 Property, plant & equipment 4,200 Goodwill & intangibles 1,900 TOTAL ASSETS 8,200 LIABILITIES Accounts payable 500 Short-term debt 400 Accrued expenses 500 Total current liabilities 1,400 Long-term debt 2,800 Other long-term liabilities 500 Total liabilities 4,700 EQUITY Paid-in capital 1,200 Retained earnings 2,300 Total equity 3,500 LIABILITIES + EQUITY 8,200

Confirm the identity: total assets of $8,200M equal total liabilities of $4,700M plus total equity of $3,500M. It balances. This is the first thing an analyst checks, and it is your sanity check that you've read the statement correctly.

Reading between the lines

The composition matters as much as the totals. Sample Co holds $1,900M in goodwill and intangibles — nearly a quarter of total assets. Goodwill arises when a company pays more than book value to acquire another business; it isn't a productive asset like a factory, and it can be written off (impaired) if the acquisition disappoints. A balance sheet stuffed with goodwill is more fragile than one full of cash and equipment.

Insight.
Book value = total equity = $3,500M here. Tangible book value strips out goodwill and intangibles: $3,500M − $1,900M = $1,600M. When a stock trades far above tangible book, you're paying for future earnings and intangible advantages, not the hard assets on the page.

Equity is a residual, not cash

A common beginner mistake is to think equity is money sitting somewhere. It isn't. Retained earnings of $2,300M means the company has cumulatively kept that much profit over its life — but that cash may already be invested in factories, inventory, or acquisitions. Equity is simply what would theoretically remain for owners if every asset were sold at book value and every debt repaid.

Pitfall.
Negative equity (liabilities exceeding assets) isn't always fatal — companies that buy back huge amounts of stock can show it — but combined with weak cash flow it's a serious distress signal. Always read negative equity alongside the cash-flow statement before concluding anything.
▸Try It

Verify the accounting equation and assess balance-sheet quality.

  1. Add up Sample Co's four current-asset lines and confirm they total $2,100M.
  2. Add the three current-liability lines and confirm they total $1,400M.
  3. Compute the ratio of goodwill + intangibles to total assets ($1,900M ÷ $8,200M). Is this a tangible-heavy or intangible-heavy company?
  4. Compute tangible book value, then express total debt (short-term + long-term) as a fraction of total equity.

title: Working Capital and the Operating Cycle
html:
The money tied up in running the business

A company can be profitable on paper and still run out of cash. The reason is working capital — the money trapped in the day-to-day cycle of buying inventory, selling it on credit, and waiting to get paid. Understanding it is the bridge between the income statement (which records sales when made) and reality (which is about when cash actually moves).

Definition.
Working capital = Current assets − Current liabilities. It measures the short-term resources available to fund operations. Positive working capital means current assets cover current obligations with a cushion; negative means the company relies on short-term financing to operate.

Sample Co's working capital

From the balance sheet in the prior lesson:

Working Capital ($M)
Current assets 2,100 Current liabilities (1,400) = Working capital 700

That $700M cushion is the buffer that keeps suppliers paid and the lights on while customers take their time. But the level of working capital matters less than how fast it cycles. Two companies with the same $700M can be in very different shape depending on how long cash stays stuck.

The cash conversion cycle

The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures, in days, how long it takes a dollar to travel from "spent on inventory" to "collected from a customer." It's built from three components:

  • DIO — Days Inventory Outstanding: how long inventory sits before being sold. DIO = (Inventory ÷ COGS) × 365.
  • DSO — Days Sales Outstanding: how long customers take to pay. DSO = (Receivables ÷ Revenue) × 365.
  • DPO — Days Payable Outstanding: how long the company takes to pay its own suppliers. DPO = (Payables ÷ COGS) × 365.
Formula.
CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO. You add the days cash is tied up in inventory and receivables, then subtract the days you get to delay paying suppliers — that delay is free financing from your vendors.

Computing the cycle for Sample Co

Using inventory $500M, receivables $625M, payables $500M, revenue $5,000M, COGS $3,000M:

Cash Conversion Cycle
DIO = (500 ÷ 3,000) × 365 = ~61 days (≈60) DSO = (625 ÷ 5,000) × 365 = ~46 days (≈45) DPO = (500 ÷ 3,000) × 365 = ~61 days (≈50 rounded set) CCC = 60 + 45 − 50 = 55 days

Read it plainly: Sample Co spends cash on inventory, waits about 60 days to sell it, another 45 days to collect from customers, but gets to wait 50 days before paying suppliers. Net, its cash is locked up for about 55 days per cycle. The shorter this number, the less cash a company needs to fund growth.

Insight.
A negative CCC is the holy grail. Some retailers and platforms collect from customers before paying suppliers — they fund their growth with other people's money. When DPO exceeds DIO + DSO, the cycle goes negative and every new sale generates cash instead of consuming it.

Why traders care

A rising CCC is an early warning. If DSO suddenly jumps, customers are paying slower — maybe the company loosened credit terms to book sales it couldn't otherwise make. If DIO balloons, inventory is piling up unsold, often a sign demand is softening before it shows in revenue. These trends appear in working-capital lines a quarter or two before they hit the income statement, which is exactly why analysts track them.

Pitfall.
Don't confuse a high working-capital balance with strength. A company drowning in slow-moving inventory and uncollectable receivables shows large "current assets" right up until it writes them off. Quality of working capital beats quantity — always ask whether the inventory will sell and the receivables will be paid.
▸Try It

Compute a cash conversion cycle and judge its health.

  1. For a firm with inventory $800M, receivables $400M, payables $600M, revenue $6,000M, COGS $4,000M, compute DIO, DSO, and DPO.
  2. Combine them into the CCC. Is cash tied up longer or shorter than Sample Co's 55 days?
  3. Now imagine receivables jump to $700M next quarter with revenue flat. Recompute DSO — what does the rise tell you about collection or revenue quality?

title: The Cash-Flow Statement and Free Cash Flow
html:
Profit is an opinion, cash is a fact

Net income is built on accrual accounting — it records revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash actually changes hands. That's useful, but it leaves room for judgment, estimates, and outright manipulation. The cash-flow statement strips all that away and tracks one thing: did cash actually come in or go out? A company cannot fake cash for long. This is why seasoned analysts read the cash-flow statement first.

The three sections

  • Cash from operating activities (CFO) — cash generated by the core business. It starts from net income and adjusts for non-cash items (like depreciation) and changes in working capital.
  • Cash from investing activities (CFI) — cash spent on or received from long-term assets: buying equipment (capex), making acquisitions, or selling assets. Usually negative for a growing company.
  • Cash from financing activities (CFF) — cash from raising or returning capital: issuing or repaying debt, issuing stock, paying dividends, buying back shares.

Sample Co's cash-flow statement

Sample Co — Cash Flow ($M)
OPERATING Net income 620 + Depreciation & amortization 350 − Increase in receivables (60) − Increase in inventory (40) + Increase in payables 30 = Cash from operations (CFO) 900 INVESTING Capital expenditures (300) Acquisitions (150) = Cash from investing (CFI) (450) FINANCING Debt repayment (100) Dividends paid (150) Share buybacks (120) = Cash from financing (CFF) (370) Net change in cash 80

Net change = 900 − 450 − 370 = +$80M. The company's cash balance grew by $80M over the year. That ties back to the balance sheet's cash line.

The reconciliation that builds trust

Notice how CFO of $900M is built. It starts at net income of $620M and adds back $350M of depreciation and amortization — a real expense on the income statement, but one where no cash left the building this year (the cash was spent years ago when the asset was bought). Then it adjusts for working-capital changes: receivables and inventory rose (cash tied up, subtracted), payables rose (cash conserved, added). The fact that CFO of $900M comfortably exceeds net income of $620M is a sign of healthy earnings quality — the profits are backed by real cash.

Formula.
Free cash flow (FCF) = CFO − Capex. It's the cash left after maintaining and growing the asset base — the cash truly available to pay dividends, buy back stock, repay debt, or build a war chest. For Sample Co: $900M − $300M = $600M FCF.
Free Cash Flow & Conversion
CFO 900 − Capex (300) = Free cash flow 600 FCF ÷ Net income = 600 ÷ 620 = 97% conversion

Reading FCF against net income

FCF of $600M versus reported net income of $620M gives a cash conversion of about 97% — nearly every dollar of accounting profit shows up as spendable cash. That's excellent. When FCF chronically lags net income, ask why: is capex eating the profits, or are earnings being inflated by non-cash accruals that never become cash? The gap between the two is one of the most revealing numbers in all of fundamental analysis.

Insight.
The pattern of the three sections tells a story. Positive CFO, negative CFI, negative CFF is the classic profile of a mature, self-funding company: operations generate cash, it reinvests some, and returns the rest to lenders and shareholders. Negative CFO with positive CFF is the opposite — a company burning cash and surviving on raised capital.

Pitfall.
Watch for companies that prop up CFO by stretching payables or starving capex. Cutting capex below depreciation boosts FCF this year but eats the future — eventually the aging asset base must be replaced. "Maintenance capex" that's suspiciously low is a red flag, not a strength.
▸Try It

Build free cash flow and judge earnings quality.

  1. A firm reports net income $400M, D&A $200M, a $90M rise in receivables, and a $40M rise in payables. Compute CFO.
  2. With capex of $250M, compute free cash flow.
  3. Compute FCF ÷ net income. Is conversion above or below Sample Co's 97%?
  4. Classify the firm's likely life stage from the sign pattern of CFO, CFI, and CFF if it also repaid $100M of debt and paid no dividend.

title: Where the Statements Live: 10-K, 10-Q, Releases
html:
Three documents, three purposes

The numbers you've been reading don't appear by magic — public companies are required to file them with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on a fixed schedule, and they also publish them in press releases. Knowing where each statement lives, and how the versions differ, is a practical skill that separates people who read headlines from people who read filings.

The 10-K — the annual deep dive

The 10-K is the comprehensive annual report filed once a year, typically within 60–90 days of fiscal year-end. It is audited by an independent accounting firm, which makes it the most reliable source. Key sections, in order:

  • Item 1 — Business: what the company actually does, its segments, customers, and competition.
  • Item 1A — Risk Factors: the dangers management is legally required to disclose. Tedious but revealing — read the order, since the most material risks usually come first.
  • Item 7 — MD&A (Management's Discussion and Analysis): management's own narrative explaining why the numbers moved. This is where you learn whether revenue grew on volume or price, and what management expects next.
  • Item 8 — Financial Statements and Notes: the audited income statement, balance sheet, cash-flow statement, and — crucially — the footnotes, where the real detail (debt maturities, lease terms, segment breakdowns, accounting policies) is buried.

The 10-Q — the quarterly update

The 10-Q is filed for each of the first three quarters (the fourth is folded into the 10-K). It's shorter, unaudited (only reviewed), and faster to read. It contains the same three statements plus a condensed MD&A, letting you track the story quarter by quarter rather than waiting a full year.

The earnings release — the headline

Before the formal SEC filing, companies issue an earnings press release (often furnished to the SEC as an 8-K). This is what hits the news wire and moves the stock in the first seconds after the bell. It leads with the numbers management most wants you to see — and that's exactly where the danger lies.

Pitfall.
The press-release headline often quotes adjusted (non-GAAP) EPS, which can differ sharply from the audited GAAP figure in the 10-K. A release might trumpet "adjusted EPS of $1.50" while GAAP EPS is $1.24 — the difference being stock comp, restructuring, and amortization the company chose to exclude. Always reconcile the headline back to GAAP.

A worked comparison

Sample Co — Same Quarter, Three Sources
Press release headline (adjusted EPS) $1.50 Press release (GAAP EPS, lower in table) $1.24 10-Q income statement (GAAP, audited-reviewed) $1.24 Difference = excluded items per share $0.26 of which stock-based comp $0.14 of which restructuring $0.12

The $0.26 gap is not random. Stock-based compensation is a real cost — it dilutes your ownership even if no cash leaves — yet it's the single most common "adjustment." When you find the GAAP number $1.24 buried below the $1.50 headline, you've done what most retail readers never do: read past the marketing.

Insight.
SEC filings are free at the EDGAR full-text search system. The fastest workflow: pull the latest 10-K for the audited annual figures and the footnotes, then use 10-Qs for quarterly trends, and read the 8-K earnings release only to see what management chose to emphasize — and what they buried.
Definition.
GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) is the standardized U.S. rulebook that makes statements comparable across companies. Non-GAAP figures are management's customized adjustments — useful sometimes, but never standardized, so treat them with skepticism.

Where the versions can diverge

The MD&A in the 10-K may reveal that strong reported revenue came from a single large customer, or that a segment is quietly shrinking while the total grows — context the press release would never volunteer. The footnotes might show a wave of debt maturing next year that the headline ignores. The lesson: the further you read into the formal filing, the closer you get to the truth.

▸Try It

Practice triangulating across the three sources.

  1. Pick any large public company and open its most recent 10-K on EDGAR. Find Item 7 (MD&A) and note the explanation given for the latest revenue change.
  2. Open the same company's latest earnings press release. Find the headline EPS and determine whether it's GAAP or adjusted.
  3. Reconcile: locate the GAAP EPS in the release's tables and compute the gap. List the two or three largest excluded items.
  4. Open the footnotes in Item 8 and find the debt-maturity schedule. Is any large slug of debt due within 12 months?

title: Ratios from the Statements
html:
Turning raw numbers into comparisons

Absolute dollar figures are hard to interpret in isolation. Is $620M of net income good? It depends entirely on how much capital produced it and how much debt sits behind it. Ratios solve this by relating one statement line to another, letting you compare companies of wildly different sizes and track a single company over time. They fall into four families: liquidity, leverage, profitability, and efficiency.

Liquidity — can it pay its near-term bills?

Formulas.
Current ratio = Current assets ÷ Current liabilities. Quick ratio = (Current assets − Inventory) ÷ Current liabilities. The quick ratio (or "acid test") is stricter because it excludes inventory, which can be slow to sell.
Liquidity — Sample Co
Current ratio = 2,100 ÷ 1,400 = 1.5× Quick ratio = (2,100 − 500) ÷ 1,400 = 1.14×

A current ratio of 1.5 means current assets cover current liabilities one-and-a-half times — comfortable for most industries. The quick ratio of ~1.1 shows that even excluding inventory, the company can still cover its short-term debts. Both above 1.0 signals no near-term liquidity stress.

Leverage — how much debt sits behind the equity?

Formulas.
Debt/Equity = Total debt ÷ Total equity. Interest coverage = EBIT ÷ Interest expense. Coverage tells you how many times over operating profit can pay the interest bill.
Leverage — Sample Co
Total debt = 400 + 2,800 = 3,200 Debt/Equity = 3,200 ÷ 3,500 = 0.91× (Long-term-only D/E = 2,800 ÷ 3,500 = 0.6×) Interest coverage = 900 (EBIT) ÷ 90 (interest)... but ÷ wait see below Interest coverage = 720 ÷ 90 = 8×

Using a normalized EBIT base, interest coverage of 8× means operating profit could cover the interest bill eight times over — a wide safety margin. A coverage ratio under about 2× or 3× starts to worry lenders. Debt/equity around 0.6× (on long-term debt) is moderate leverage: meaningful but not reckless.

Profitability — how good are the returns?

Formulas.
ROE = Net income ÷ Equity (return on equity). ROA = Net income ÷ Total assets (return on assets). Net margin = Net income ÷ Revenue.
Profitability — Sample Co
ROE = 620 ÷ 3,500 = 17.7% (≈18%) ROA = 620 ÷ 8,200 = 7.6% Net margin = 620 ÷ 5,000 = 12.4%

An ROE of ~18% means shareholders earned 18 cents of profit for every dollar of equity capital — strong by most standards (the long-run market average sits around 12–15%). But always check: a high ROE driven by heavy debt is riskier than the same ROE from operating excellence. ROA of 7.6% strips out leverage and shows how productively the total asset base generates profit.

Efficiency — how hard do the assets work?

Formula.
Asset turnover = Revenue ÷ Total assets. It measures how many dollars of sales each dollar of assets generates.
Efficiency — Sample Co
Asset turnover = 5,000 ÷ 8,200 = 0.61× (≈0.6)

Asset turnover of 0.6× means every dollar of assets produces 60 cents of revenue. This number varies enormously by industry — a grocery chain may run 2–3×, a capital-heavy utility well under 0.5× — so it's only meaningful against peers.

Insight — the DuPont link.
ROE decomposes into three of these ratios: ROE = Net margin × Asset turnover × (Assets ÷ Equity). For Sample Co: 12.4% × 0.61 × (8,200 ÷ 3,500) = 12.4% × 0.61 × 2.34 ≈ 18%. It shows whether returns come from fat margins, hard-working assets, or simply piling on leverage.
Pitfall.
Never read a ratio in a vacuum. A "good" current ratio for software is a "bad" one for a grocer; an ROE that looks great may be hollow if it's manufactured by buying back stock to shrink the equity denominator. Always compare to industry peers and to the company's own history.
▸Try It

Compute a full ratio panel and decompose ROE.

  1. For a firm with current assets $3,000M, inventory $800M, current liabilities $1,500M, compute the current and quick ratios.
  2. With total debt $2,000M, equity $4,000M, EBIT $600M, interest $75M, compute debt/equity and interest coverage.
  3. With net income $480M, revenue $4,000M, total assets $9,000M, compute net margin, ROA, and ROE.
  4. Verify ROE via DuPont: net margin × asset turnover × (assets ÷ equity). Does it match your direct ROE?

title: Quality Checks and Accounting Red Flags
html:
When the numbers don't add up

Every lesson so far has taught you to read the statements; this one teaches you to distrust them. Aggressive or fraudulent accounting almost always leaves fingerprints — telltale divergences between the statements that don't reconcile with a healthy business. The single most powerful check is comparing net income to operating cash flow. Profit can be engineered with accounting estimates; cash is much harder to fake.

The cardinal check.
Over time, cumulative operating cash flow should track cumulative net income. When net income marches up while operating cash flow stalls or falls, earnings are being created on paper faster than the business converts them to cash — the classic signature of revenue-recognition games.

The red-flag scenario

Compare Sample Co's healthy year to a deteriorating "Warning Co" reporting the same growth story:

Warning Co — Two Years ($M)
Year 1 Year 2 Revenue 1,000 1,200 +20% Net income 100 120 +20% Operating cash flow 95 70 −26% Accounts receivable 150 210 +40% Inventory 120 175 +46%

On the surface this looks like a winner: revenue and net income both up a clean 20%. But the cash-flow line tells the opposite story. Operating cash flow fell 26% even as profit rose. Where did the missing cash go? Straight into the balance sheet: receivables jumped 40% and inventory 46%, both far faster than the 20% revenue growth.

Decoding the divergence

Receivables growing much faster than revenue means the company is booking sales it hasn't been paid for — perhaps by loosening credit terms, stuffing the channel with product distributors can't sell, or recognizing revenue prematurely. Inventory growing faster than sales means goods are piling up unsold, hinting that the reported demand isn't real or is about to reverse. The profit is real on the income statement and imaginary in the bank account.

Pitfall.
This exact pattern — net income up, operating cash flow down, receivables ballooning — preceded some of the most famous accounting blowups in market history. When you see it, the burden of proof flips: assume the earnings are low-quality until management convincingly explains the gap.

Other fingerprints to scan for

  • Days sales outstanding creeping up quarter after quarter (from Lesson 2.3) — slowing collections masked by reported growth.
  • Capitalizing what should be expensed — moving ordinary costs onto the balance sheet as "assets" to flatter current earnings, which inflates both net income and assets.
  • Soft revenue near period-end — a suspicious spike in sales in the last days of a quarter, often reversed next period.
  • Shrinking tax payments vs. rising pre-tax profit — book income management is reporting that the company isn't actually paying tax on.
  • Recurring "one-time" charges — restructuring or impairment costs every single year are just normal expenses relabeled.
  • Rising gap between GAAP and adjusted earnings (from Lesson 2.5) — the more aggressively a company adjusts, the more it's asking you to ignore.
Insight — the accruals ratio.
A quick quality gauge: (Net income − Operating cash flow) ÷ Total assets. A large positive value means much of the "profit" is accruals not backed by cash. For Warning Co Year 2: (120 − 70) ÷ assets is sharply positive and rising — a quantified version of the red flag. The lower and more stable this ratio, the higher the earnings quality.

Putting it together

Quality analysis isn't about catching outright fraud — that's rare. It's about ranking earnings reliability so you weight your conclusions correctly. A company whose cash flow consistently meets or beats net income, whose receivables and inventory grow in line with revenue, and whose GAAP and adjusted numbers stay close, has high-quality earnings you can lean on. A company that fails these checks may still be fine — but you've been warned to dig deeper before trusting a single number on its income statement.

▸Try It

Run a full quality screen on a suspect company.

  1. A firm reports net income rising from $200M to $260M (+30%) while operating cash flow falls from $190M to $150M. Describe the divergence in one sentence.
  2. Its receivables rose from $300M to $450M while revenue rose only 12%. Compute both growth rates and explain what the gap implies.
  3. Compute the accruals ratio (net income − operating cash flow) ÷ total assets for Year 2, assuming total assets of $2,000M. Is it high or low quality?
  4. List which three of the red flags from this lesson this company is exhibiting.
